name: Tswana
description: The Tswana are a Sotho-Tswana Bantu people whose heartland spans South Africa's North West Province and the Republic of Botswana (which takes its name from them). They live in large urban-like towns (unlike the dispersed homesteads of many African peoples) — some Tswana towns had populations exceeding 10,000 before colonialism. They are governed by hereditary chiefs (dikgosi) who rule through open-air assemblies (kgotla) where any community member can speak.
cultural_highlights: Kgotla assembly — the open-air community court where all men have a right to speak; a democratic institution predating European parliaments
Dikgosi chieftaincy — hereditary chiefs govern through the kgotla; power is legitimated by consensus, not force alone
Large pre-colonial towns — Tswana towns like Dithakong and Kuruman had populations of 10,000–20,000 before European contact
Bogwera and bojale initiations — male and female initiations respectively involving circumcision, education, and preparation for adult responsibilities

food_traditions.name: Phaleche (samp)
food_traditions.description: Coarsely crushed maize kernels cooked to a thick porridge — eaten with dried meat or vegetables

food_traditions.name: Motoho (fermented porridge)
food_traditions.description: Soured sorghum porridge drunk as a probiotic beverage and eaten as a breakfast food

food_traditions.name: Setswana chicken (kgogo ya Setswana)
food_traditions.description: Free-range chicken cooked in a clay pot with minimal spicing; the flavour comes from the quality of the bird

music_traditions: Setapa dance — a communal circle dance performed by women at weddings and community celebrations
Phala flute — a notched end-blown flute played by herd boys in the veld
Sax and jazz influence — the Tswana of the Rand mines contributed significantly to South African township jazz
